Attention Hierarchy

  • ambient: Low priority. Status bars or helper text.
  • routine: Standard priority. Toasts or inline notes.
  • urgent: High priority. Banners or System Alerts.
  • critical: Blocking priority. Dialogs or Screen Takeovers.

Tone Semantics

tone is part of a unified severity/tone vocabulary shared across Message (structural), Feedback (transient), and Validation (input states). The semantic mapping aligns all three:

  • neutral / info: Informational. No action required.
  • positive / success: Confirmation. Positive outcome achieved.
  • caution / warning: Advisory. Issue detected but not blocking. User may act.
  • negative / error: Failure. Problem occurred. User action required.

name: Reliability Intent
summary: Protocol defining error recovery strategies, retry policies, and outcome handling for mechanism failures (system/operation errors). Distinct from input-invalidity (validation domain): Reliability handles operation failures (timeout, crash, network); Validation handles input errors (field wrong, value out of range).

tolerance.description: How severely the user is affected by failure

recovery.description: What the user experiences during system recovery

description:
Scope: Mechanism Failure (not Input Invalidity)

Reliability is strictly for operation/system failure, not validation errors:

  • Input-invalidity (Validation domain): "The user's email is malformed" or "These passwords don't match". This is an expected outcome; the field is wrong, and the user must fix it. Validation owns this.
  • Mechanism failure (Reliability domain): "The uniqueness check timed out" or "The server is unreachable" or "The async validator crashed". This is an unexpected system problem. Reliability owns this.

Boundary with Loader: a wait that is merely taking too long but still running is a Loader Intent concern (its escalation dimension: warn, then go-async). Reliability takes over only once that wait times out or fails — it owns the timeout default (configurable per operation class) and what the user experiences during recovery.

A validation scenario that includes async (e.g., checking if an email is already registered) may emit a Reliability error if the check itself fails (server down), but a Validation error if the email is taken. The distinction prevents conflating the two error families.

Failure Tolerance

How severely the user is affected when an operation fails. This determines the visual response and the user's ability to continue.

  • forgivable: Operation failed but the user can continue. Communicated silently (logged) or with minimal disruption (toast). App state remains valid.
  • degraded: Partial failure. The UI renders with missing data or fallback content. The user can still interact but the experience is incomplete.
  • terminal: Broken state. The component cannot render meaningfully. Requires replacement with an error boundary or a full-page error state.

Recovery Experience

What the user experiences while the system attempts to recover from a failure. The implementing block decides the technical retry strategy (timing, backoff algorithm).

  • automatic: The system retries silently. The user sees a loading state but takes no action.
  • manual: The user is presented with a "Retry" affordance and decides when to try again.
  • none: No recovery is attempted. The failure state is final.

Fallback Content

What the user sees in place of the failed content.

  • skeleton: Structural placeholder, implying the content may load later.
  • empty: Explicit empty state with messaging (e.g., "No data available").
  • previous: Stale data from the last successful load, marked as outdated.

Interface Protocol

type FailureTolerance = 'forgivable' | 'degraded' | 'terminal';
type RecoveryExperience = 'automatic' | 'manual' | 'none';
type FallbackContent = 'skeleton' | 'empty' | 'previous';

interface ReliabilityIntent {
    // How severely the user is affected
    tolerance: FailureTolerance;
    // What the user experiences during recovery
    recovery: RecoveryExperience;
    // What replaces the failed content
    fallback?: FallbackContent;
}

name: Type-Ahead Intent
summary: Protocol for keyboard type-ahead navigation: typed characters move focus to the matching item, with a buffer that resets after an idle timeout. Distinct from filtering.

matching.description: How typed characters map to items

reset.description: When the accumulated character buffer clears

wrap.description: Focus behavior at the ends of the collection

description:
Matching Strategy

How typed characters are mapped to an item. Type-ahead moves focus to an existing item — it never removes or hides items (that is filtering, see aria-autocomplete="list" on a combobox).

  • prefix: Characters accumulate into a buffer; focus moves to the first item whose label starts with the buffer.
  • cycle: A repeated single character cycles focus through items whose label starts with that character.
  • prefix-then-cycle: Recommended default. Matches WAI-ARIA APG and native <select> — characters typed in rapid succession accumulate into a prefix, while a lone repeated key cycles among same-initial items.

Reset Timing

When the character buffer is discarded so the next keystroke starts a fresh search.

  • debounced: Buffer clears after an idle timeout (defaults to 500ms, the APG / native <select> convention).
  • custom: Application-defined timeout via resetMs.

Wrap Behavior

What happens when matching passes the last item.

  • wrap: Search wraps around to the start of the collection (default).
  • clamp: Search stops at the ends without wrapping.

Label Source

The text each item is matched against. Defaults, in order: aria-label, the element referenced by aria-labelledby, then textContent. Applications may override via a label resolver.

Interface Protocol

type TypeAheadMatching = 'prefix' | 'cycle' | 'prefix-then-cycle';

interface TypeAheadIntent {
    // How typed characters map to items
    matching: TypeAheadMatching;
    // Idle ms before the buffer resets (default 500)
    resetMs: number;
    // Wrap focus past the ends of the collection (default true)
    wrap: boolean;
    // Override how an item's searchable label is derived
    label?: (item: Element) => string;
}

name: Locale Intent
summary: Protocol defining the application's active locale, writing direction, and locale-bound format preferences. The app-wide preference axis the Localization Protocol consumes; retroactively fills the RTL/format gaps implied by temporal, typography, and anchor.

locale.description: The active locale as a BCP-47 language tag (open-valued — not a fixed enum)

direction.description: The writing direction the layout flows in

numbering.description: Optional override for the numbering system (defaults to the locale's CLDR default)

calendar.description: Optional override for the calendar system (defaults to the locale's CLDR default)

description:
Active Locale

The current locale as a BCP-47 language tag. This is an open axis — the standard fixes the shape (a valid tag) and the negotiation rules, not a closed list of locales. Components read the active locale to format values (via Intl) and resolve messages (via the Localization Protocol).

Writing Direction

The direction the layout flows in. Drives the dir attribute, the :dir() pseudo-class, and CSS logical properties so components adapt without bidirectional CSS forks.

  • ltr: Left-to-right (Latin, Cyrillic, CJK).
  • rtl: Right-to-left (Arabic, Hebrew). Inline start/end flip; physical left/right do not.
  • auto: Direction inferred per the Unicode bidi algorithm from the content's first strong character (matches dir="auto").

Format Preferences

Optional overrides for locale-bound formatting, defaulting to the locale's CLDR defaults. These complement the Temporal and Typography intents, which previously had no locale axis to defer to.

  • numbering: Unicode numbering system (a nu subtag value) — e.g. latn, arab, deva.
  • calendar: Unicode calendar system (a ca subtag value) — e.g. gregory, islamic, japanese.

Interface Protocol

type Direction = 'ltr' | 'rtl' | 'auto';

interface LocaleIntent {
    // Active BCP-47 language tag (open-valued)
    locale: string;
    // Writing direction the layout flows in
    direction: Direction;
    // Optional Unicode numbering system (nu subtag)
    numbering?: string;
    // Optional Unicode calendar system (ca subtag)
    calendar?: string;
}

name: Translation Intent
summary: Protocol defining how a locale change is delivered to the UI from a UX standpoint — instant (all catalogs preloaded, synchronous switch), async (lazy-load the target catalog behind a loading state), or reload (full per-locale document load). Orthogonal to the Locale Intent: locale declares which locale; translation declares how switching to it feels.

switching.description: How a locale change manifests in the UI, trading bundle/payload size against switch latency and client-state preservation

description:
Switching Strategy

How changing the active locale is delivered to the user. This is a UX and runtime-cost decision, independent of which locale is active (that is the Locale Intent). The implementing block decides the mechanism; this axis declares the experience the application wants.

  • instant: All candidate catalogs are preloaded (bundled or prefetched) before the switch. Changing locale is synchronous — no network round-trip, no loading state, no navigation; client state is preserved. Best for a small number of locales or compact catalogs. Cost: upfront payload grows with every supported locale.
  • async: The target locale's catalog is lazy-loaded on demand when the user switches. A loading state bridges the fetch and the UI updates in place when it resolves; client state is preserved. Smallest initial payload. Cost: a network round-trip on first switch to a locale — design the loading affordance via the Loader Intent.
  • reload: The switch is delivered by a full document load — navigating to a per-locale URL (/fr/…) or a per-locale build (SSR / static). There is no in-place reactive update; the new locale arrives with a fresh document. Best for SEO/SSR and compile-time per-locale builds. Cost: a full navigation that discards transient client state.

Composition

Each strategy leans on a neighbouring standard rather than re-solving it:

  • instant → preload the candidate catalogs ahead of need; eagerness composes with the Prefetch Intent.
  • async → the catalog fetch surfaces a pending state via the Loader Intent.
  • reload → the per-locale document is delivered by URL-based locale routing (Router).

Interface Protocol

type TranslationSwitching = 'instant' | 'async' | 'reload';

interface TranslationIntent {
    // How a locale change is delivered to the UI
    switching: TranslationSwitching;
}

name: Collection Operations Intent
summary: UX-only protocol for the four data operations on a collection — filter, sort, group, page — composed as a configurable pipeline (default filter → sort → group → page). Sort's vocabulary mirrors the platform verbatim: direction follows aria-sort (ascending/descending), text comparison follows Intl.Collator (sensitivity/numeric/caseFirst/ignorePunctuation); group aggregates follow SQL naming. Models the front-end only — comparator implementation and client/server execution are technical concerns handled outside the intent. INITIAL vocabulary, pending owner review.

pipelineOrder.description: Which data operations run and in what order. Configurable; default filter → sort → group → page (sort the filtered set, group the sorted set, page the grouped set). Each stage is optional and independent.

sortDirection.description: Sort direction for a key — named to match the native aria-sort attribute the active header projects. An empty sort projects aria-sort="none".

sortKeys.description: Whether sort keys may be stacked — ties on the first key break on the next (shift-click multi-column sort).

textSensitivity.description: Which character differences affect text order. Adopts the Intl.Collator sensitivity option verbatim.

numericOrdering.description: Natural numeric order so embedded numbers compare by value (img2 before img10). Adopts the Intl.Collator numeric option.

caseFirst.description: Which case sorts first. Adopts the Intl.Collator caseFirst option.

ignorePunctuation.description: Whether punctuation is ignored when comparing. Adopts the Intl.Collator ignorePunctuation option.

emptyPlacement.description: Where empty / missing values land in the order. Mirrors SQL NULLS FIRST / NULLS LAST — the web platform has no native term.

filterMatch.description: How active filter predicates combine — AND or OR. The predicates themselves are call-site data, not part of the intent.

groupSummary.description: The aggregate shown per group, named after the SQL aggregate functions (the nearest cross-platform standard).

pageMode.description: The navigation model — does advancing replace the slice or grow the list. paged = discrete numbered / prev-next page links that replace the visible slice (the only model that supports jump-to-page and a known total); append = each new slice accumulates onto a growing list. Orthogonal to the advance trigger below: 'infinite scroll' is not a third mode, it is append + advance:auto.

advance.description: How the next slice is requested — orthogonal to pageMode. manual = an explicit user action (clicking a page link or a Load-more button); auto = the next slice fetches automatically at the scroll boundary, via the native IntersectionObserver sentinel. Default manual: auto-advance is the opt-in that turns append into infinite scroll, and it carries the documented infinite-scroll harms (unreachable footer, no stopping point), so it is never the default — on a full-page collection. Bounded-surface members override this: the dropdown/autocomplete family pins advance:auto as its default because a listbox popover has no footer to strand and every surveyed combobox auto-loads at the scroll boundary (autocomplete member, ratified #018 Fork 2); a clicked "Load more" row remains the manual opt-in and the keyboard/screen-reader fallback. paged + auto is undefined (you do not auto-replace on scroll).

urlSync.description: Whether the active page is reflected in a shareable, back-button-restorable URL (e.g. ?page=n). Native-first: the History API (pushState/popstate/scrollRestoration) is the baseline; the Navigation API is a progressive enhancement; for server-rendered paged links, cross-document View Transitions animate page-to-page with zero JS. Load-bearing: append modes (especially advance:auto) are only advisable when this is on, else the footer and back button break.

rangeLabel.description: Whether to surface the slice as a 'showing 21–40 of 500' range label. Format is {from}–{to} of {total} {noun} (en-dash, numbers via Intl.NumberFormat). Requires a known total, so it composes fully only over an offset/page resource protocol; under a cursor protocol (no total) it degrades — drop the total and show 'showing 21–40' (with next driven by hasNextPage), or '…of more than {to}' when a floor is known. Never 'of many'. A11y mandate: render the label in a role=status + aria-atomic=true live region so the new range is announced after a page change (W3C ARIA22, sufficient for WCAG 2.1 SC 4.1.3). The label sits outside the pagination nav landmark.

pageSize.description: Whether the user can change the items-per-page. fixed = a single app-set page size; selectable = a page-size selector (a native labeled <select>, e.g. 'Items per page'). When selectable, default to persisting the user's choice across sessions (NN/g: respect the stated preference as the next default); the storage mechanism (URL query param / localStorage / server profile) is app-owned. The size value itself is call-site data (page.size); there is no canonical option ladder — 10/25/50/100 is a defensible default, but NN/g research favours one sensible default (10 or 20) and at most two options. Cap large sizes / 'All' (~100) for performance and lower the ceiling on mobile.

description:
The Pipeline

Four data operations compose as an ordered pipeline over a collection. Each stage is optional and independent; the order is configurable, defaulting to filter → sort → group → page (you sort the filtered set, group the sorted set, page the grouped set). This intent is UX-only — it expresses what the user wants, never how it is computed. The vocabulary below is an initial pass pending review; where the platform already has a word for a concept, this intent borrows it verbatim rather than inventing one.

Sort — direction & comparison

Direction names match the native aria-sort attribute the active header projects (an empty sort is aria-sort="none"). The text-comparison preferences adopt the Intl.Collator option vocabulary verbatim.

  • sortDirection — ascending | descending (aria-sort).
  • sortKeys — single | multiple: may sort keys be stacked.
  • textSensitivity — base | accent | case | variant (Intl.Collator sensitivity).
  • numericOrdering — natural numeric order so img2 precedes img10 (Intl.Collator numeric).
  • caseFirst — upper | lower | false (Intl.Collator caseFirst).
  • ignorePunctuation (Intl.Collator ignorePunctuation).
  • emptyPlacement — first | last: where empty / missing values land (mirrors SQL NULLS FIRST/NULLS LAST; the web has no native term).

Filter, Group & Page

  • filterMatch — all | any: combine active predicates with AND or OR; the predicates are call-site data.
  • groupSummary — count | sum | average | minimum | maximum: named after the SQL aggregate functions.
  • pageMode — paged | append: navigation model. paged replaces the slice (numbered / prev-next, the only model with jump-to-page and a known total); append grows the list.
  • advance — manual | auto: trigger, orthogonal to pageMode. manual (default) is a click; auto fetches at the scroll boundary via IntersectionObserver. Infinite scroll = append + auto — a derived opt-in, not a third mode; rendering delegates to Windowed Collection, lifecycle to Loader. paged + auto is undefined.
  • urlSync — none | query-param: whether the active page is a shareable, back-button-restorable URL. append modes are only advisable with query-param on.
  • rangeLabel — none | range: show ‘showing 21–40 of 500’ ({from}–{to} of {total}, en-dash, Intl.NumberFormat). Needs a known total → composes fully only over an offset/page protocol; under cursor it degrades to ‘showing 21–40’ (or ‘…of more than {to}’), never ‘of many’. A11y: render in a role="status" + aria-atomic live region (W3C ARIA22 → WCAG 4.1.3); sits outside the pagination nav.
  • pageSize — fixed | selectable: may the user change items-per-page (a labeled native <select>). When selectable, persist the choice across sessions by default (NN/g); the size value is call-site data and the option ladder is app-owned — 10/25/50/100 is a defensible default, though NN/g research favours one sensible default and few options. Cap large sizes / ‘All’ and lower the mobile ceiling.

Page — native primitives, SEO & a11y

The pagination dimensions stay UX-only; the offset/page-vs-cursor protocol lives in the Custom Pagination resource, not here. A cross-layer constraint binds them: jump-to-page and rangeLabel require a total, so they pair only with an offset/page protocol — a cursor protocol forces append or prev-next. URL state aligns to native primitives: the History API (pushState/popstate/scrollRestoration) as baseline, the Navigation API as enhancement, and CSS @view-transition for zero-JS server-rendered transitions. SEO: the indexable default is server-rendered, self-canonical ?page=n links — Googlebot does not fire the user-action JS behind append/advance:auto, and rel=next/prev is deprecated (Google, 2019), so never emit it as primary machinery. A11y: paged controls live in a <nav aria-label="pagination"> landmark with aria-current="page" on the active link (WAI-ARIA APG); focus & announcement after a page change are still open. See the collection-operations research and the 2026-06-03 pagination research report for the full pattern decision framework.

UX-only — where the technical choices live

Deliberately not in this intent: the comparator implementation (default Array.prototype.sort vs Intl.Collator vs a numeric comparator vs decorate-sort-undecorate), and where the work runs. This standard models the front-end only; delegating to a backend ORDER BY / sort query is app-owned and out of scope. Those technical strategies, with their trade-offs, are catalogued in the collection-operations research and Plateau's Technical Configurator (Sorting Strategy domain).

Composition

This intent owns only the data operations; neighbours own the rest:

  • Windowed Collection — renders the visible window; this selects/orders the set. Compose, never merge.
  • Locale — supplies the default collation locale, so a French app sorts French by default.
  • Loader — append paging surfaces its loadingMore state (and at the advance:auto scroll sentinel).
  • Selection — co-resident on the same grids; an orthogonal axis.
  • Action — its groupOrdering is prior art for a declarative ordering dimension.

Interface Protocol

type SortDirection = 'ascending' | 'descending';            // aria-sort
type Sensitivity = 'base' | 'accent' | 'case' | 'variant';  // Intl.Collator
type CaseFirst = 'upper' | 'lower' | 'false';               // Intl.Collator
type EmptyPlacement = 'first' | 'last';                     // SQL NULLS FIRST/LAST
type Operation = 'filter' | 'sort' | 'group' | 'page';

interface SortKey {
    field: string;
    direction: SortDirection;
    // Comparison preferences mirror Intl.Collator option names verbatim
    sensitivity?: Sensitivity;
    numeric?: boolean;            // natural numeric order: img2 before img10
    caseFirst?: CaseFirst;
    ignorePunctuation?: boolean;
    emptyPlacement?: EmptyPlacement;
}

interface CollectionOperationsIntent {
    // Stages applied in order; configurable, default below
    order: Operation[];           // default ['filter','sort','group','page']
    sort?: { by: SortKey[]; keys: 'single' | 'multiple' };
    filter?: { match: 'all' | 'any' };
    group?: { summary: Array<'count' | 'sum' | 'average' | 'minimum' | 'maximum'> };
    page?: { mode: 'paged' | 'append'; size?: number;
             pageSize?: 'fixed' | 'selectable';  // user-changeable items-per-page; persist when selectable
             advance?: 'manual' | 'auto';        // default manual; append+auto = infinite scroll
             urlSync?: 'none' | 'query-param';   // History API ?page=n
             rangeLabel?: 'none' | 'range' };     // 'showing 21-40 of 500' — offset/page only; role=status
}

designSystemResearch.keyInsight: aria-sort standardizes the sort STATE vocabulary on a column header (one header at a time); this intent's sortDirection projects onto it directly.
The standard locale-aware comparison engine; this intent adopts its option names verbatim so the UX preference is expressed in the platform's own vocabulary rather than a bespoke one.
Models the four operations as ordered, optional, composable row-model transformers — the architectural precedent for a configurable pipeline of independent stages.

designSystemResearch.system: History API / Navigation API / View Transitions
designSystemResearch.keyInsight: The page dimension's urlSync aligns to native URL-state primitives rather than a router library: History API (Baseline 2020) is the baseline for ?page=n + back/forward + scroll restoration, the Navigation API (Baseline 2026) is the SPA-grade enhancement, and cross-document View Transitions animate server-rendered paged navigation with zero JS.

SEO and a11y constrain paged pagination's defaults: each page needs a unique self-canonical URL of server-rendered links (Googlebot does not fire load-more JS; rel=next/prev deprecated 2019), and controls belong in a labeled nav landmark with aria-current='page'.

researchGaps: Pagination focus & announcement — what receives focus and what is announced after a page change is unspecified (the verified a11y part is only the nav landmark + aria-current). Tracked at /backlog/059-pagination-focus-announcement/.

Pagination range labels & page-size selectors — 'showing 21–40 of 500' and page-size options have no verified best-practice (sizes, defaults, persistence). Tracked at /backlog/060-pagination-range-labels-page-size/.

Per-dimension UX vocabulary completeness — this is an initial pass. Validate the value lists against a real grid/list corpus and provide a custom escape hatch for non-standard preferences (open-system stance), since the bar is 'describe any UX preference'.
Pipeline-order validity — order is configurable; are some orders (e.g. page-before-filter) nonsensical and rejected, or is it free-form?
Server boundary residual — execution is front-end only; should the standard still recommend a canonical sort/filter serialization so apps converge, even though it does not own the wire format?

Shared comparison primitive — does sort order + dirty-check equality + locale collation share one primitive (Web Intl)? Tracked in the technical layer / the Configurator's Sorting Strategy domain, not in this UX intent.

Provisional surface details — the event names (sortchange/filterchange/groupchange/pagechange) are placeholders with no native precedent, and the boolean-valued dimensions (numericOrdering, ignorePunctuation) render as true/false to mirror Intl.Collator. Confirm or rename during owner review.

name: Background Task Intent
summary: Protocol defining the persistent surface that hosts long-running work after it goes async — the home a Loader hands a task to past its escalation threshold. Covers task registration that outlives the originating view, aggregation of concurrent tasks with per-task progress and retry, and the navigation-away / before-unload guard. Distinct from the Feedback Intent (transient toasts) and the Loader Intent (in-view pending state).

aggregation.description: How concurrent backgrounded tasks are presented in the surface

persistence.description: How long a completed task's entry remains in the surface

navigationGuard.description: Whether an in-flight task warns the user before they navigate away or close

description:
What This Is

The Loader Intent's escalation: async value says that past a longer platform-defined duration a still-running task should go async: a persistent status surface stays present, the user is free to navigate away, and a window prompt warns before they close or leave (the task continues in the background). The Loader declares that this should happen; the Background Task Intent defines the surface that hosts those backgrounded tasks — the receiving end of that handoff.

It is a distinct UX surface, not a loader. It outlives the originating view, aggregates multiple concurrent tasks, shows per-task progress, and mediates the navigation-away warning. It is not the Feedback Intent (transient, fire-and-forget toasts) and not the Loader Intent (in-view pending state that dies with its view).

Platform anchor: the browser's Background Fetch API models exactly this shape — long transfers that survive navigation, with browser-provided progress UI — but this intent's scope is broader than fetches (any long task), so it borrows the vocabulary without requiring a service worker.

Aggregation

How concurrent backgrounded tasks present in the surface.

  • single: One task at a time. The surface shows a single progress entry.
  • batch: Multiple concurrent tasks, each with its own progress and retry — one task's failure does not abort the others (unless they are dependent). Mirrors the Loader Intent's determinate guidance for parallel uploads.

Persistence

How long a completed task's entry remains in the surface.

  • transient: The entry auto-clears once the task resolves successfully (optionally emitting a Feedback toast on completion).
  • sticky: The entry stays until explicitly dismissed. The default for error entries, so a task that failed while the user was on another view is still reviewable and retryable when they return.

Navigation Guard

Whether an in-flight task arms the close / route-leave warning.

  • none: No prompt; the task continues silently in the background and the user leaves freely.
  • warn: While any task is active, leaving the app fires the beforeunload prompt and an in-app route change shows a confirm dialog. Cleared automatically when the last in-flight task resolves.

Note: the “warn before you leave with work still in flight” behavior is the same paradigm as “warn before discarding unsaved form edits.” That cross-cutting guard is tracked for harvest as its own composable intent; this intent will compose it rather than redefine it.

Composition (cross-reference, do not duplicate)

  • Loader: the upstream source. A task arrives here via Loader's escalation: async. Per-task progress reuses Loader's determinate/indeterminate indicator, version/generation token, and lifecycle states — it is the same state machine, hosted off-view.
  • Reliability: owns timeout and failure policy. Retry of an individual failed task delegates here; this surface only exposes the affordance.
  • Layout: the surface is an app-shell region (a dock). Placement and region mechanics defer to the Layout Intent.
  • Feedback: downstream. A completed task may emit a transient toast; Feedback does not track ongoing state, this intent does.

Lifecycle

A task entry in the surface moves through:

  • registered: A task has been handed off (escalated async) and now has an entry.
  • active: Work in progress; arms the navigation guard and shows progress.
  • success: Resolved; entry behaves per persistence.
  • error: Failed; entry stays (sticky) and exposes retry (via Reliability).
  • dismissed: Removed from the surface by the user or by auto-clear.

Interface Protocol

type TaskAggregation = 'single' | 'batch';
type TaskPersistence = 'transient' | 'sticky';
type TaskNavigationGuard = 'none' | 'warn';
type BackgroundTaskState = 'registered' | 'active' | 'success' | 'error' | 'dismissed';

interface BackgroundTaskIntent {
    // How concurrent tasks present in the surface
    aggregation: TaskAggregation;
    // How a completed entry persists
    persistence: TaskPersistence;
    // Whether in-flight work warns on navigate-away / close
    navigationGuard: TaskNavigationGuard;
}

interface BackgroundTask {
    id: string;
    label: string;            // sentence case, -ing verb, ≤ 3 words (Loader label conventions)
    state: BackgroundTaskState;
    // Per-task progress mirrors the Loader Intent
    progress: 'determinate' | 'indeterminate';
    value?: number;           // 0–100 when determinate
    retry?: () => void;       // present on error; delegates to Reliability
}

name: Reorder Intent
summary: UX-only protocol for user-mutable order of a collection — grab (whole or handle), move by pointer or keyboard, commit ephemerally or persistently, announced via live region. Manual order, distinct from Collection Operations' computed sort. Keyboard parity is part of the contract, not an add-on.

grab.description: What the user grabs to initiate a move

movement.description: Input modalities for moving an item. Keyboard is non-optional — 'both' is the conformance baseline

commit.description: Whether the new order is written somewhere durable or lives only for the session

announce.description: How each move and the final commit are announced to assistive tech

scope.description: Reorder within one list, or move items between sibling lists

description:
What Reorder Is

Reorder makes the order of items in a collection user-mutable. The item set is unchanged — only their sequence changes. This is manual order, distinct from the Collection Operations sort dimension, which produces a computed order from a comparator. The two are mutually exclusive answers to "what determines order": a list is sorted or hand-ordered, not both at once.

Reorder is one of two families under "drag and drop". The other — moving a payload (item, file) into a zone that validates what it accepts (data transfer) — is tracked separately under gap-11.

Grab

  • whole: Default. The entire item is the grab affordance.
  • handle: Only a scoped handle within the item initiates a move, leaving the rest of the item interactive (links, buttons).

Movement

Reorder must be operable by pointer and keyboard. Keyboard parity is part of the contract, not an enhancement — a pointer-only reorder is non-conforming.

  • pointer: Drag via Pointer Events (mouse, touch, pen), unified through pointer capture.
  • keyboard: Roving tabindex over items, grab/move/drop keys, with live-region announcement.
  • both: Recommended baseline. Both paths present and equivalent.

Commit

  • ephemeral: Default. The new order lives for the session only.
  • persisted: The new order is written somewhere durable (localStorage, or written back to the source via an event the host handles).

Announce

The deprecated aria-grabbed/aria-dropeffect attributes are not used. Announcement is via a live region instead.

  • live-region: Default. Each move and the final commit are announced (e.g. "Moved item 3 to position 1 of 8"), composing with the Live Region Status intent.
  • none: No announcement (only valid when no keyboard/AT path is exposed — discouraged).

Scope

  • within-list: Default. Items reorder inside a single list.
  • cross-list: Items move between sibling lists that share a group key.

Composition

Reorder composes with Interaction (pointer/touch modality), Focus Delegation (roving tabindex for the keyboard path), and Live Region Status (announcement). A reorderable item card pairs with the preserve-on-move capability so an atomic Element.moveBefore() relocation keeps the item's focus, animation, and connection state.

name: Disclosure Intent
summary: Show/hide of a content region via a toggle control — expand/collapse, exclusive accordion grouping, and find-in-page reveal. Built on the native <details>/<summary> substrate.

surface.description: Where the disclosed panel renders (#609 Fork 1A). in-flow expands the panel in document flow (the accordion baseline — pushes siblings down); anchored lifts it to an out-of-flow surface anchored to its trigger (the reveal-nav / mega-menu / dropdown recipe — composes the anchor intent with strategy=escape, type=menu, so it overlays without reflow).

defaultState.description: Initial expanded state of the region

multiplicity.description: How grouped panels behave: exclusively (one open at a time) or independently

findable.description: Whether collapsed content is still revealed by browser find-in-page

description:
What Disclosure Is

Disclosure makes a content region's visibility user-toggleable through a labelled control — the show/hide widget the WAI-ARIA APG Disclosure pattern names. Expand/collapse panels, accordions, and "read more" sections are all disclosure. The native substrate is <details>/<summary>: the summary is the toggle (exposing aria-expanded), the rest of the element is the region, and the open attribute is the state. No JavaScript is required for the base behavior.

Default State

  • closed: Default. Region starts collapsed (bare <details>).
  • open: Region starts expanded (<details open>).

The values mirror the platform exactly: a ToggleEvent's newState is literally the string "open" or "closed".

Multiplicity

  • independent: Default. Each panel toggles on its own; any number can be open at once (ungrouped <details>).
  • exclusive: Panels sharing a group key form an exclusive accordion — opening one closes its siblings. The native mechanism is a shared <details name="…">; exclusive is the HTML spec's own term for this grouping.

Findable

  • false: Default. Collapsed content is removed from rendering and not searchable.
  • true: Collapsed content is hidden with hidden="until-found", so browser find-in-page still matches it and auto-expands the region (firing beforematch).

Animated Reveal — Composed, Not Redefined

Disclosure stays about state, not animation. An animated expand/collapse is supplied by composing the Motion Intent rather than re-specifying timing here — mirroring how the Modal Intent composes Focus Containment instead of redefining focus. The native height animation is pure CSS over the ::details-content pseudo-element using interpolate-size: allow-keywords and transition-behavior: allow-discrete, which is exactly Motion's territory.

Composition

Disclosure composes with Motion (animated reveal, honoring prefers-reduced-motion) and, for the toggle control's modality, Interaction. It does not own focus management — the summary is a normal focusable control.

Interface Protocol

type DisclosureState = 'open' | 'closed';
type Multiplicity = 'exclusive' | 'independent';

interface DisclosureIntent {
    // Initial expanded state (maps to the `open` attribute)
    defaultState: DisclosureState;
    // Exclusive accordion grouping (maps to <details name>) vs independent
    multiplicity: Multiplicity;
    // Reveal collapsed content to find-in-page (maps to hidden=until-found)
    findable: boolean;
    // Optional animated reveal — see the Motion Intent
    motion?: MotionIntent;
}

name: File Revision Intent
summary: UX-only protocol for the distinguishing trait of a file-UPDATE control over a plain upload: a value already exists, so the control owns revert-to-original and the moment the staged replacement becomes the displayed value. Models presence as an axis (valuePresence: existing vs empty) so file-upload and file-update are the SAME control at two settings — the droplist-style answer to 'is update a distinct standard or just upload's under-modeled current-value dimension'. Entry/accept (file-source, drop-target, accepts) is the data-transfer intent's territory (#007); transfer/commit/persistence are technical and live in the File Upload & Update configurator domain, not here. INITIAL vocabulary, pending owner review.

valuePresence.description: Whether a current value already exists when the control mounts — the single axis that separates UPDATE from UPLOAD. 'existing' unlocks revert and the swap model below; 'empty' degrades the same control to a plain upload with no original to preserve. Modeling it as a dimension is the claim that upload and update are one component, not two.

swapModel.description: When the displayed value flips from the current file to the staged replacement. 'confirmed' (default) keeps showing the original until the commit succeeds — the data-safe choice when an existing value is precious; 'optimistic' shows the replacement immediately and rolls back to the original on failure. UX-only: where the bytes actually go (endpoint / OPFS / File System Access handle / form value) is the technical commit strategy, handled in the configurator.

revert.description: Whether restoring the original value is offered while a replacement is staged (the affordance a greenfield upload has no concept of). 'enabled' (default) lets the user back out to the pristine value before committing; 'disabled' makes the replacement one-way once staged. Pairs with the observable revision state pristine → staged → committed that drives both the control's affordances and its live-region announcements.

description:
What File Revision Is

A file update control is not a new widget — it is a plain file upload that starts with a current value. That one precondition is the whole intent: because a value already exists, the control owns semantics a greenfield upload never needs — revert-to-original and a deliberate choice about when the staged replacement becomes the displayed value. File Revision names exactly that delta and nothing else, so a bare <input type="file">, a drag-and-drop dropzone, and a resumable chunked uploader are the same control at different trait altitudes (the droplist decomposition, applied to files).

Presence Is the Axis

The open question 'is file-update a distinct standard, or just the observation that the upload component always had an under-modeled current-value dimension?' is answered by making presence a dimension: valuePresence: existing is an update, empty is an upload, and they are one component. existing is what unlocks revert and the swap model; empty degrades cleanly to the platform default.

Scope — UX Only

Per the intent rule, this models the user-facing concern only. The technical axes a real upload needs are deliberately not dimensions here:

  • How files enter (native picker / dropzone / paste / directory / File System Access handle) and what is accepted (type / size / count) are the data-transfer intent's drop-target / accepts contract (#007) — File Revision composes it, never redefines it.
  • How bytes move (single / chunked / resumable) and where they are written (POST/PUT endpoint, OPFS, FS handle, form value) are technical strategies, operationalized as the File Upload & Update domain in the Technical Configurator — not intent vocabulary.
  • Resumability (the IETF Resumable Uploads draft / the de-facto tus protocol) maps onto a Reliability recovery handler (resume = retry-from-offset), so it belongs there rather than in a bespoke transfer trait.

Composition

File Revision composes, rather than re-specifies: the data-transfer intent (#007) for the source affordance and accept-constraint; the Validation intent for pre-transfer gating of type/size/count; the Loader / Background Task intents for the idle → selected → transferring → done | error progress lifecycle and its live-region announcements; and Reliability for resumable transfer. Its own contribution is purely the revision delta below.

Native Substrate

The always-keyboard-operable baseline is <input type="file"> (with accept, multiple, capture, webkitdirectory); the File API (File/Blob/FileList) carries the bytes. A bare input has no notion of a current value, so the revision state (pristine vs staged) is the component's to track — but every higher trait must degrade back to that operable input, and progress-state changes must announce via a live region. This intent mandates the a11y floor; it does not bolt it on.

Interface Protocol

type ValuePresence = 'existing' | 'empty';
type SwapModel = 'confirmed' | 'optimistic';
type RevisionState = 'pristine' | 'staged' | 'committed';

interface FileRevisionIntent {
    // Does a current value exist at mount? 'existing' = update, 'empty' = upload.
    valuePresence: ValuePresence;
    // When the staged replacement becomes the displayed value.
    swapModel: SwapModel;          // default 'confirmed'
    // Offer restore-to-original while a replacement is staged.
    revert: boolean;               // default true
    // Observable revision lifecycle — drives affordances + announcements.
    readonly state: RevisionState;
    // Source affordance + accept-constraint are composed from the data-transfer intent (#007),
    // not owned here; transfer + commit strategy are technical (see the configurator).
}

name: Autofocus-on-Activation Intent
summary: Where initial keyboard/AT focus lands when a surface becomes active — a route swap, tab change, or overlay open — by a closed set (heading | target | preserve | auto). The shared landing atom that Focus Containment and Navigation compose.

landing.description: Where initial keyboard/AT focus lands when the surface becomes active

description:
The landing question

When a surface becomes active — a route swap, a tab change, a modal/overlay opening — initial keyboard/AT focus must land somewhere by a closed set. This intent owns that one question and nothing else: a surface just became active, where does initial focus land? It is the accessibility twin of the Navigation scroll dimension (where the viewport lands); both are latent behaviors of rendering a new surface.

Landing

  • heading (default): the new surface's main heading, made programmatically focusable (tabindex="-1" + .focus()). The WAI-ARIA APG answer and the overwhelmingly correct default.
  • target: an author-named element inside the surface (e.g. the first field of a wizard step), referenced by id/ref.
  • preserve: leave focus where it is — correct for in-place swaps that do not relocate the user (a filter re-render, an in-place tab panel).
  • auto: platform default — honor a URL fragment or a descendant autofocus attribute, else fall back to heading, aligned with the native dialog-focusing steps so native behavior is not overridden gratuitously.

Composition

The same landing question fires on every surface activation, so neither composer owns a private copy of the value set:

  • Focus Containment = this atom (landing) + trap + restore-on-close; its initialFocus dimension is the overlay profile of this landing contract.
  • Navigation composes the landing only — a forward navigation does not make the rest of the page inert.

It is not a Focus Delegation axis: roving within a live widget presupposes a delegator, and a view swap has none. Ruling carried from #056 / #287.

Interface Protocol

type Landing = 'heading' | 'target' | 'preserve' | 'auto';

interface AutofocusOnActivationIntent {
    // Where initial keyboard/AT focus lands when the surface becomes active
    landing: Landing;
    // Element reference (id/selector), when landing is 'target'
    target?: string;
}

designSystemResearch.keyInsight: On activating a new view/surface, set focus deliberately — the APG default is the new surface's main heading made focusable (tabindex=-1 + focus()). This is the heading default this intent adopts.
The platform already lands focus via a descendant autofocus attribute, the dialog-focusing steps, and fragment targets — the auto value defers to these before falling back to heading (native-first).
Headless libraries expose initial-focus as a standalone mount-time concern separate from trap/restore — validating landing as a composable atom rather than a property baked into the modal.

name: Data-Transfer Intent
summary: The typed-payload + accepts contract behind the native DataTransfer object shared by clipboard and drag events: a payload (text, rich content, files, app items) entering a zone that declares what it accepts, plus the copy/cut/paste copy-out half. Drop-target and file-source compose it.

flow.description: Direction of transfer across the zone — into it, out of it, or both

payload.description: The typed content category the DataTransfer carries; a zone's accepts list is expressed over these

acceptance.description: How a receiving zone constrains an incoming payload

description:
One object, two surfaces

The browser moves a payload through a single native object — DataTransfer — exposed as event.clipboardData on clipboard events and event.dataTransfer on drag events. This intent names the recurring contract that rides on it: a typed payload entering a zone that declares what it accepts, plus the programmatic copy-out half (navigator.clipboard, ClipboardItem). It is UX vocabulary only — the native objects and any provider sit behind it.

Flow

  • receive: payload moves in — a paste or a drop onto the zone.
  • emit: payload moves out — a copy or cut from the zone (the half neither drop-target nor file-source owns).
  • both: the zone accepts and offers — e.g. an editor region that pastes in and copies out.

Payload

The typed categories a transfer carries; a zone's accepts list is a subset of these, mirroring the multiple MIME entries a single DataTransfer exposes.

  • text: plain string (text/plain).
  • rich: structured/markup content (text/html, text/uri-list, and other format strings).
  • files: one or more File objects (DataTransfer.files / a pasted/dropped file).
  • items: application-defined data carried under a custom format string (a dragged row, a card, an app entity).

Acceptance

  • declared: the zone publishes an allow-list of payload categories; a payload outside it is rejected (and the rejection is surfaced, not silently dropped). The validation contract the neighbours share.
  • any: the zone takes whatever arrives and sorts it out itself.

Composition

This intent is composed, not used bare — it is the substrate its consumers share so the accepts contract is defined once:

  • Drop-target (the drag-and-drop trait family, #022) is a receive zone with a declared accepts list — it composes this rather than re-deriving payload/accepts validation.
  • File Revision's file-source paste/drop providers consume it for the clipboard-paste and drop ingestion paths.
  • The clipboard emit (copy/cut) surface rides here as the copy-out half — grounded in navigator.clipboard.write / ClipboardItem — which neither neighbour previously owned.

Interface Protocol

type Flow = 'receive' | 'emit' | 'both';
type Payload = 'text' | 'rich' | 'files' | 'items';
type Acceptance = 'declared' | 'any';

interface DataTransferIntent {
    // Into the zone (paste/drop), out of it (copy/cut), or both
    flow: Flow;
    // Typed categories the transfer may carry
    payload: Payload[];
    // Whether a receiving zone validates against a declared accepts list
    acceptance: Acceptance;
    // The declared allow-list, when acceptance is 'declared'
    accepts?: Payload[];
}

designSystemResearch.keyInsight: Clipboard and drag-and-drop share one DataTransfer object exposing typed entries by format string plus a files list — the single substrate this intent names, so the typed-payload + accepts contract is defined once across both surfaces.
The programmatic copy-out/paste-in half — ClipboardItem carries multiple typed representations, matching the payload categories — is the emit flow that drop-target and file-source do not own.

A drop zone signals acceptance via dragover/dropEffect against the dragged types — the receive + declared-accepts behavior, validating what it takes before the drop, that this intent factors out of the drop-target trait.

name: Exit Guard Intent
summary: UX vocabulary for confirming before a region the user is in is torn down while it holds unsaved or in-flight work. The exit-guard member of the Guard protocol: arms on the cancelable dismissal of any region (document, route, overlay); the deny-outcome is a user-mediated confirm (stay / discard / save). The loss-of-state predicate is supplied by the guard provider, never by the intent.

scope.description: Which region's teardown is guarded — scale-invariant. document is the outermost region (the whole page leaving); route is a same-document navigation; overlay is an in-app dismissal (modal, drawer, panel).

condition.description: What the user is warned they would lose — the user-facing flavour of the loss-of-state predicate. unsaved = uncommitted edits (dirty form, editor); in-flight = work still running (an upload, a background task).

resolution.description: The user-mediated outcomes the confirm offers. stay cancels the dismissal and keeps the region; discard proceeds and abandons the work; save commits the work first, then proceeds.

presentation.description: How the confirm is shown. native = the browser's own generic, non-customizable prompt (the only option at document scope, via beforeunload); custom = an in-app dialog with bespoke copy and a save affordance (available for route and overlay scopes).

description:
Exit Guard Contract

When the user makes an intentful, cancelable attempt to leave or dismiss a region — closing the tab, navigating to a new route, dismissing a modal — and that region holds outstanding work, a confirm is presented before the teardown completes. The user, not the app, decides whether to proceed. Whether work is outstanding is decided by the guard provider; this intent only declares that a confirm is presented when it is, never how the condition is computed.

Per-scope vocabulary

  • document: the whole page is closing or reloading. The browser shows its own generic prompt (beforeunload) — the app cannot customize the text or offer a Save button. presentation is forced to native here.
  • route: a same-document navigation away from the current view. The app intercepts the navigation and can show a custom dialog with bespoke copy.
  • overlay: an in-app dismissal of a modal, drawer, or panel. The cancelable close action is intercepted; a custom dialog is shown.

Resolution

  • stay: cancel the dismissal; the region and its work remain.
  • discard: proceed; the outstanding work is abandoned.
  • save: commit the work, then proceed (custom presentations only — the native prompt cannot offer this).

What this intent does not cover

Only cancelable dismissal triggers are guardable — a close button, a route change, beforeunload. Imperative teardown that bypasses a guardable trigger (an ancestor calling el.remove(), a framework unmount) happens too late to confirm and is out of scope. The predicate, its provider, and the trust/enforcement boundary live in the Guard protocol, not here.

Composition

  • Built on the Guard protocol — the sibling of the entry-guard / access member (access control); both delegate their predicate to a guard provider, but the exit guard's deny-outcome is a confirm rather than hide / redirect / forbid / cloak.
  • The Form block and any unsaved-editor surface reference this intent rather than re-implementing a dirty-check prompt.
  • The Background Task Intent's navigation-guard dimension collapses to referencing this intent for the in-flight case.

uxResearch.title: Exit-guard prior art across frameworks and platforms
uxResearch.source: Web Everything navigation-guard / Guard protocol research (#129, 2026-06-10)
findings: Every major router exposes a leave-guard: Angular CanDeactivate, Vue beforeRouteLeave, React Router useBlocker, SvelteKit beforeNavigate — all gate a transition on a predicate and let the user cancel.
The browser baseline is beforeunload, which arms the document scope but can only render a generic, non-customizable prompt — no custom copy and no Save affordance.
Native platforms confirm the same pattern at overlay scope: iOS presentationControllerShouldDismiss / isModalInPresentation guard an interactive dismissal against a dirty state.

The unifying abstraction is guarding a region's teardown against a loss-of-state predicate, not 'navigation' — which is why this is the exit-guard member of the surface-agnostic Guard protocol, not a router-only feature.
uxResearch.keyInsight: Routing leave-guards, beforeunload, and native modal-dismissal guards are one intent at three scopes; the only real UX divergence is that the document scope is stuck with the browser's native prompt.

researchGaps: Reconcile a custom route-scope confirm with the browser's native beforeunload prompt when a single user action triggers both (e.g. a hard reload mid-route) — the user must not see two stacked prompts.
Whether 'save' belongs in this intent's resolution vocabulary or composes from a separate Save/Action intent, given the native scope structurally cannot offer it.
How the confirm copy is sourced per condition (unsaved vs in-flight) without the intent reaching into the provider's predicate internals.

name: Access Control Intent
summary: UX vocabulary for the entry member of the Guard protocol — declarative app-level authorization. Two surfaces share one authz provider: a routing entry guard (deny -> a navigation outcome) and a rendering access gate (deny -> render or hide a subtree). The deny-outcome family is hide | redirect | forbid | cloak; the forbid(403)-vs-cloak(404) existence-disclosure call lives behind the provider, not in the UX. Feature-flags resolve as an authority kind alongside authorization / process / validity. The front end is a UX mirror; the back end is authoritative.

surface.description: Where authorization is evaluated — the two surfaces sharing one provider. route is a navigation entry guard (deny cancels/redirects the navigation); render is an element-level access gate (deny hides or replaces a subtree).

deny.description: What happens on a deny — the UX names the family, the provider owns the 403-vs-404 call. redirect sends elsewhere (route only); hide removes the subtree (render only); forbid acknowledges the resource but refuses (403); cloak hides its very existence (404). forbid is downgraded to cloak unless the provider reveals existence.

authority.description: Why access is gated — the authority kind, an open set defaulting to authorization. authorization = permanent, data-scoped permission; feature-flag = temporary entitlement/rollout (same provider seam, materially different stakes); process = a workflow-state gate; validity = a data-validity precondition.

description:
Access Control Contract

The settled entry mirror of the exit guard: when the user tries to reach a protected region — navigating to a route, or rendering a gated subtree — a guard provider decides allow/deny, and on deny the configured deny-outcome fires. Built on the Guard protocol's predicate-provider seam; it is not a new enforcement contract.

Two surfaces, one provider

  • route: a navigation entry guard — deny cancels the navigation (the native substrate is the Navigation API navigate intercept) and applies redirect / forbid / cloak.
  • render: an element-level access gate — deny applies hide / forbid / cloak to the subtree.

They are not the same control; they share only the predicate — one authz provider feeds both.

Deny-outcome family

hide | redirect | forbid | cloak. The load-bearing security choice — forbid (403, confirms the resource exists) vs cloak (404, hides existence) — is decided behind the provider, not by the UX author: RFC 9110 sanctions answering 404 to hide a forbidden resource, and leaking existence enables enumeration. A requested forbid is concealed to cloak unless the provider reveals existence.

Authority kinds

Access is gated by an authority kind — an open set (authorization, feature-flag, process, validity), default authorization. Feature-flags are a first-class authority kind, not separate machinery: a flag SDK is just one guard provider and inherits the identical trust boundary.

Trust boundary

Every evaluation is async and back-end-authoritative; this intent is a UX mirror, never the enforcement point, and a grant is revocable. A client-side gate — including a feature-flag one — is never a sufficient control on its own. The predicate, its provider, and the enforcement boundary live in the Guard protocol, not here.

uxResearch.title: Access-control authorization-gate prior art across frameworks and platforms
findings: Authorization is universally factored into two surfaces — a route guard (Angular CanActivate -> UrlTree, React Router loader throw redirect, Vue navigation guards) and an element-level render gate — fed by one shared ability/policy source.

The entry-deny outcomes collapse to redirect / block / render-a-forbidden-or-not-found view; the load-bearing one is forbid(403)-vs-cloak(404), which RFC 9110 makes a security (existence-hiding) call, not a styling one.

OPA/CASL show the structure is exactly the provider seam: the guarded surface is a Policy Enforcement Point that queries a swappable Policy Decision Point; the member consumes the verdict and bakes no policy model.

Feature-flags and authorization abstract the same gate but differ in stakes and permanence, so feature-flag is an authority kind resolved through the same provider, never a license to enforce on the client.
uxResearch.keyInsight: The member is two surfaces off one authz provider; the UX names the deny family but the 403-vs-404 existence call and the enforcement both live behind the provider, server-authoritative.

researchGaps: How a render gate's cloak (existence-hiding) interacts with SSR/streaming — concealing a subtree must not leak its existence via timing or partial markup.
Whether the authority kind should drive a default deny-outcome (e.g. feature-flag -> hide, authorization -> cloak) or stay orthogonal to it.

name: Hierarchy Intent
summary: The nesting paradigm — role=tree › treeitem › role=group with aria-level/setsize/posinset, Right/Left expand-then-descend / collapse-then-ascend traversal, and the flatten-to-visible-rows projection that lets Windowed Collection virtualize a tree unchanged. Composes Disclosure for per-node expand state; delegates Selection, Focus-Delegation, and Type-Ahead. Tree-select, treegrid, and nested menus compose this one intent.

selectableNodes.description: Which nodes may be committed to the selection (default: any; leaf is an opt-in constraint)

cascade.description: Parent/child selection propagation, with indeterminate ancestors on partial selection — gated to selection.model = multiple (default: off)

valueReporting.description: Which nodes a cascaded selection reports as its value (Ant showCheckedStrategy) — gated to selection.model = multiple (default: all)

description:
What Hierarchy Is

Hierarchy is the nesting paradigm — a tree of parent and child nodes and the rules for traversing them. It is the structure named by the WAI-ARIA APG Tree View pattern: a role=tree whose treeitems wrap their children in a role=group, each item carrying aria-level, aria-setsize, and aria-posinset. Tree-select, and future treegrid and nested-menu surfaces, all compose this one intent rather than re-deriving nesting.

The platform separates three independent axes, and so does this intent:

  • Disclosure (open/closed, aria-expanded) — per-node expand state is the Disclosure Intent, composed here, not redefined. A node's expanded/collapsed state is exactly <details>-style disclosure.
  • Hierarchy (this intent) — the nesting structure itself: levels, traversal, and the flatten-to-visible-rows projection.
  • Selection (aria-selected) — which nodes are chosen is delegated to the Selection Intent; selection is independent of disclosure (a collapsed folder can be selected).

Traversal & Keyboard

The APG keyboard model is adopted verbatim — Right/Left carry both disclosure and horizontal movement, while Up/Down never change expand state:

  • Right — expand-then-descend: a closed node opens (focus stays); an open node moves to its first child; an end node does nothing.
  • Left — collapse-then-ascend: an open node closes; a child end/closed node moves to its parent; a root end node does nothing.
  • Up / Down — move through the visible (focusable) set only; they never open or close.
  • Home / End, type-ahead, * (expand siblings) — standard. Type-ahead is delegated to the Type-Ahead Intent.

Windowing — Flatten to Visible Rows

Hierarchy owns a projection: the tree is flattened to a linear array of currently-visible rows (nodes whose ancestors are all expanded), and that flat list is virtualized like any other. Hierarchy survives only as per-row metadata (level, posinset, setsize, expanded); expand/collapse just changes the array's length. So the Windowed Collection Intent needs zero tree-specific changes — there is no tree-specific offset math.

The load-bearing rule is focus, not offsets: focus management is delegated to Focus-Delegation as a DI-injectable strategy (virtual aria-activedescendant for family consistency, or roving tabindex for very large trees). Either way, collapsing a node moves focus to the collapsing ancestor, guaranteeing the focused node is always present in the visible projection — so a focused node can never be stranded inside a collapsed branch.

Selectable Nodes

  • any: Default. Any node may be selected, at any depth — the APG model and the cross-library consensus (a collapsed parent is selectable).
  • leaf: An author opt-in constraint restricting commit to leaf nodes (Shoelace's selection="leaf"). Never the default.

Cascade (multi-select only)

When Selection is model: multiple, checking a node can propagate to related nodes, with partially-selected ancestors shown indeterminate. Gated to multi-select; ignored under single-select.

  • off: Default. Each node is selected on its own (MUI's decoupled default); no propagation.
  • descendants: Checking a node selects all of its descendants.
  • ancestors: Checking a node updates its ancestors' indeterminate/selected state.
  • both: Full checkbox-tree cascade — descendants follow, ancestors reflect (Ant treeCheckable, Fluent, PrimeReact checkbox mode).

Value Reporting (multi-select only)

Which nodes a cascaded selection reports as its value — Ant's showCheckedStrategy. Also gated to model: multiple.

  • all: Default. Report every selected node (SHOW_ALL).
  • leaves: Report only fully-selected leaves, collapsing covered subtrees (SHOW_CHILD).
  • parents: Report a fully-selected parent in place of its children (SHOW_PARENT).

Composition

Hierarchy composes the Disclosure Intent for per-node expand state (no new disclosure work), and delegates Selection, Focus-Delegation, and Type-Ahead. There is no native tree substrate — <select><optgroup> cannot nest and <details> has no selection model — so a hierarchy widget is an ARIA-composed custom element; the native-first baseline degrades to a single-level grouped <select>.

Interface Protocol

type SelectableNodes = 'any' | 'leaf';type Cascade = 'off' | 'descendants' | 'ancestors' | 'both';type ValueReporting = 'all' | 'leaves' | 'parents';interface HierarchyIntent {    // Which nodes may be committed (default: any; leaf is the opt-in constraint)    selectableNodes: SelectableNodes;    // Parent/child propagation — only meaningful when selection.model === 'multiple'    cascade: Cascade;        // default: off    // Which nodes the cascaded value reports (Ant showCheckedStrategy) — multiple only    valueReporting: ValueReporting;  // default: all    // Per-node expand state is composed from the Disclosure Intent    disclosure: DisclosureIntent;    // Selection, focus strategy, and type-ahead are delegated to their own intents    selection: SelectionIntent;    focus: FocusDelegationIntent;    // DI-injectable: virtual | roving}

name: Command Intent
summary: The declarative surface for invoking an action — which commands exist, their keybindings, and command-palette behaviour. Grounded on the Baseline Invoker Commands API (command/commandfor + CommandEvent); a keybinding engine (Mousetrap, tinykeys) is an adapter, never the baseline. Orthogonal to the Action Intent (visual weight); the net-new dependency the Menu block composes.

binding.description: The keyboard shape that triggers the command (default: none)

scope.description: Where the command and its binding are active (default: contextual)

discoverability.description: Whether the command appears in the command palette (default: listed)

description:
What Command Is

A command is the declarative surface for invoking an action — which commands exist, the keyboard shortcuts that trigger them, and how they surface in a command palette. The native substrate is the Invoker Commands API (Baseline 2025): a control declares commandfor + command, and activating it dispatches a CommandEvent on the target (carrying .command and .source). Built-in commands cover popovers and dialogs; an app-defined command uses a ---prefixed value and handles its own CommandEvent.

Command is not the Action Intent. Action is about an element's visual weight (primary/destructive); Command is about invocation (what fires, by what key, found where). They are orthogonal and compose: a destructive button can invoke a command. And a keybinding engine (Mousetrap, tinykeys, hotkeys-js) is an adapter, never the baseline — the platform's key handling and CommandEvent are native-first; an engine is the optional opt-in (settled in #016).

Binding

The keyboard shape that triggers the command — using the established keybinding vocabulary:

  • none: Default. No dedicated shortcut; the command is invoked by click, menu item, or the palette. Most commands are this.
  • chord: A single simultaneous combination (e.g. Ctrl+K).
  • sequence: An ordered multi-key sequence (the g then i style).

Scope

  • contextual: Default. The command (and its binding) is active only while its surface or element has focus. The safe, composable default — it cannot collide app-wide.
  • global: Active application-wide regardless of focus. A deliberate opt-in: a global binding claims an app-wide shortcut and can shadow other contexts, so it is never the default.

Discoverability

  • listed: Default. The command appears in the command palette, so it is discoverable and searchable by name. The most permissive default — every command is findable unless deliberately hidden.
  • hidden: The command is invocable (by key or programmatically) but omitted from the palette — for internal or redundant commands.

Composition

Command is the one net-new dependency the Menu family (#173) composes — every menuitem invokes a command. A toggle command (e.g. "Toggle Sidebar") does not own its on/off state: it composes Selection or Disclosure for the state and stays a pure invocation here. Visual prominence composes the orthogonal Action Intent; modality of the trigger composes Interaction. The command palette itself is a surface that lists listed commands and dispatches the chosen one.

Interface Protocol

type Binding = 'none' | 'chord' | 'sequence';type Scope = 'contextual' | 'global';type Discoverability = 'listed' | 'hidden';interface CommandIntent {    // Stable command identifier (maps to the `--`-prefixed CommandEvent.command value)    id: string;    // Human label shown in menus and the palette    label: string;    // Keyboard shape that triggers it (default: none)    binding: Binding;    // Where the binding is active (default: contextual)    scope: Scope;    // Whether it appears in the command palette (default: listed)    discoverability: Discoverability;}

name: Viewport Presence Intent
summary: The shared in/out-of-view trigger: observe when an element enters or leaves a reference frame, owning the observe-vocabulary (reference, margin, coverage, enter/leave) — not the UX decision (fetch, prefetch, activate) that each consumer keeps.

reference.description: The frame an element's presence is measured against (the IntersectionObserver root)

margin.description: How early presence flips relative to the reference edge (the IntersectionObserver rootMargin)

coverage.description: How much of the element must lie within the reference to count as present (the IntersectionObserver threshold)

description:
What Viewport Presence Is

Viewport Presence is the thin, shared in/out-of-view trigger — the answer to "is this element currently within a reference frame?", surfaced as enter/leave events. The native substrate is IntersectionObserver: it owns the observe-vocabulary (root, rootMargin, threshold) and nothing about what happens next.

It owns the trigger, not the UX decision. What a consumer does on presence — fetch the next page, prefetch a link, activate a visibility-gated behavior — stays with that consumer. This intent exists only so the three consumers that today re-implement the same observer (Collection Operations advance:auto, Prefetch eagerness:viewport, and the visibility-gated trait) compose one trigger instead of triplicating it — so a rootMargin-defaulting fix lands once, not three times.

Reference

The frame presence is measured against — the IntersectionObserver root.

  • viewport: Presence is measured against the browser viewport (root: null). The common case.
  • scroll-ancestor: Presence is measured against a scrolling ancestor element (e.g. a scroll container or virtualized list), passed as the observer root.

Margin

How early presence flips relative to the reference edge — the IntersectionObserver rootMargin. The exact offset is a config value the consuming surface (or the platform config it extends) sets; the dimension names the two intents.

  • flush: Presence flips exactly at the reference edge (zero margin) — react when the element is actually on screen.
  • anticipatory: Presence flips before the edge (a positive rootMargin) — fire ahead of arrival so a consumer can fetch or prefetch in advance. The most-flexible, eager framing the pre-trigger consumers want.

Coverage

How much of the element must lie within the reference to count as present — the IntersectionObserver threshold.

  • sliver: Any pixel visible counts (threshold: 0) — the most permissive.
  • partial: A small fraction must be visible before presence flips.
  • majority: More than half must be visible.
  • full: The whole element must be within the reference (threshold: 1).

Events

The two observable transitions every consumer composes:

  • enter: The element became present (crossed into the reference under the active margin + coverage).
  • leave: The element became absent (crossed back out).

Composition

Consumers compose Viewport Presence rather than inline an observer: Collection Operations keeps advance:auto (scroll-to-boundary → fetch the next slice) and delegates only the trigger; Prefetch keeps eagerness:viewport and delegates only the trigger; a visibility-gated trait activates its behavior on enter. The UX decision stays three; the observe-mechanism becomes one.

Interface Protocol

type PresenceReference = 'viewport' | 'scroll-ancestor';type PresenceMargin = 'flush' | 'anticipatory';type PresenceCoverage = 'sliver' | 'partial' | 'majority' | 'full';interface ViewportPresenceIntent {    // The frame presence is measured against (IntersectionObserver root)    reference: PresenceReference;    // How early presence flips vs the reference edge (IntersectionObserver rootMargin)    margin: PresenceMargin;    // How much of the element must be in-frame to count (IntersectionObserver threshold)    coverage: PresenceCoverage;}

name: Status Indicator Intent
summary: Canonical display of a domain entity's lifecycle state — a semantic status chip/badge plus the available next transitions — so an entity's current state, and what can happen to it next, read consistently across every surface. The visual member of the Web Lifecycle protocol.

tone.description: How the current state maps to a semantic severity (drives colour/icon, never bespoke per-screen)

shape.description: The visual metaphor for the status token

affordance.description: Whether the available next transitions are surfaced alongside the status

description:
What Status Indicator Is

The canonical, accessible presentation of where an entity is in its lifecycle — the loan that is underwriting, the claim that is approved, the order that is shipped. It is the visual member of the Web Lifecycle protocol: it reads the same lifecycle provider and renders the current state plus, optionally, the moves available from here.

It owns the display, not the rules. Which states exist and which transitions are legal belong to the lifecycle definition; whether the actor may fire one belongs to Web Guards. This intent only standardizes how the state is shown and announced — so a status stops being a hand-coloured <span> per screen.

Tone

A status maps to a semantic severity, not an ad-hoc colour. The tone (not the hex) is the intent; the theme resolves it.

  • neutral: an inert/draft state. info: informational, in-system. progress: actively moving through the flow. positive: a successful terminal/near-terminal state. caution: needs attention (conditions, on-hold). critical: declined/failed/expired.

Shape

The visual token: badge (boxed label), pill (rounded), dot (a coloured dot + text, for dense tables), or bare text.

Affordance

display-only shows the state; actionable also surfaces the available next transitions (from the provider) as controls. A11y: a state change is announced via Live Region Status rather than re-defining an announcement here.

name: Audit Timeline Intent
summary: Canonical per-entity history rendering — a chronological feed of who did what, when, and before→after — with filtering. The visual member of the Web Audit protocol; it reads the same audit provider and renders the AuditEvent stream for one entity.

detail.description: How much of each event is shown inline

description:
What Audit Timeline Is

The canonical presentation of an entity's immutable history — the loan's transitions, the conditions cleared, the documents accepted or rejected, the decision issued — as a chronological, filterable feed. It is the visual member of the Web Audit protocol: it reads the same audit provider (queryByEntity) and renders the AuditEvent stream; it does not own the log.

It owns the display, not the record. Append-only semantics, actor attribution, and before/after belong to the protocol; this intent standardizes only how that history is shown and scanned — so an audit log stops being a bespoke table per screen.

Density

compact for a dense side panel; comfortable for a full-page history.

Grouping

flat is a single stream; by-day partitions under date headers; by-actor groups consecutive events by who performed them.

Detail

summary shows actor + action + time per row; expanded also reveals the before→after change set (the Web States ChangeRecord pairs).

name: Decision Trace Intent
summary: Canonical explanation of why a rules/criteria evaluation reached its outcome — a criteria trace table (each criterion's input, operator, threshold, contributed outcome) plus the aggregated outcome and its reason codes. The visual member of the Web Decisions protocol; it renders a DecisionRecord and composes the Status Indicator intent for the outcome.

layout.description: How the criteria are laid out

disclosure.description: Whether criterion detail is shown inline or behind an expander

description:
What Decision Trace Is

The canonical answer to "why did the system decide this?" — the underwriting finding, the eligibility determination, the pricing tier, the moderation call. It is the visual member of the Web Decisions protocol: it renders a DecisionRecord as a criteria trace table (each criterion's input, operator, threshold, and contributed outcome) plus the aggregated outcome and reason codes.

It owns the explanation display, not the rules. Which criteria exist and how they evaluate belong to the decision engine (proof-of-compliance, #093); this intent only standardizes how the result is shown and announced. The outcome (and each criterion's outcome) is shown via the Status Indicator intent — one consistent status token, not a bespoke colour.

Layout

table is the dense five-column trace; list is a stacked, narrow-column form.

Emphasis

all-criteria shows every rule evaluated; deciding shows only the criteria that drove a non-pass outcome — the "why not approved" subset.

Disclosure

inline shows criterion detail in the row; expandable collapses it behind a per-row expander for long traces.

name: Master-Detail Intent
summary: Coordination between a collection (the master list) and a coupled detail region: which item is selected, how focus and announcement flow, whether the selection deep-links, and the empty/loading states. A standalone UX-coordination intent that COMPOSES existing intents — selection, layout, loader, live-region-status, focus-delegation, navigation — rather than introducing a new contract.

detailScope.description: Where the detail for the selected item lives

focusFlow.description: On select, whether focus stays on the list or moves to the detail

selectionSync.description: Whether the selected item is reflected in the URL (composes the router)

emptyState.description: What the detail region shows when nothing is selected

description:
What Master-Detail Is

The foundational enterprise layout where a list drives a coupled detail region — an inbox, a pipeline, an admin table. Selecting a row updates the detail, with the keyboard, focus, and a11y wiring that makes the pairing usable. It recurs everywhere, so the coordination is worth naming.

It is composition, not a new contract.
