Master-Detail has no provider and no schema of its own; it composes intents that already exist: selection (which item is picked), layout (the region), loader (pending detail), live-region-status (announce the change), focus-delegation (the focus flow), and navigation (deep-linking the selection). The reference master-detail block coordinates them over the shipping selection block.

Detail scope

panel couples a side/detail region; inline expands the detail within the list row; route deep-links the detail as a sub-route.

Focus flow

retain keeps focus on the list for fast keyboard scanning; advance moves focus into the detail (e.g. a form to act on).

Selection sync

none, or deep-link to reflect the selected item in the URL so it is shareable and back/forward works (composes the router).

Empty state

placeholder shows a prompt when nothing is selected; collapse hides the region.

name: Permission Intent
summary: Cross-cutting presentation of the W3C Permissions API tri-state (granted | denied | prompt) — the current status, its change events, and the request / re-prompt-recovery affordance — spanning camera, microphone, geolocation, clipboard, and notifications. State, not render: it standardizes how a permission state is surfaced and re-requested, never the feature it gates. Ratified in #009 Fork A.

state.description: The permission status, borrowed verbatim from the W3C Permissions API tokens (PermissionState). The intent never coins its own values.

affordance.description: How the UI lets the user act on the state. Programmatic re-prompt is blocked by browsers once a permission is denied — recovery routes the user to browser/OS settings rather than firing the prompt again.

feature.description: Which capability the permission gates — an OPEN vocabulary keyed off the W3C PermissionName descriptors, extended as new powerful features ship. Deliberately not notification-specific.

description:
What Permission Is

A small cross-cutting intent owning the W3C Permissions API tri-state — granted, denied, prompt — for any powerful feature (camera, microphone, geolocation, clipboard, notifications…), not one feature in particular. It standardizes how a permission’s state is shown, requested, and recovered, so the “enable camera” / “allow notifications” affordance stops being hand-rolled per screen.

State, not render

This is a state axis, explicitly not folded into feedback (a render axis — transient toasts): the breadth is far wider and the concern is orthogonal. A consumer composes permission state with whatever render surface it likes.

State

The tri-state is borrowed verbatim from the platform; the intent coins nothing. prompt = not yet decided (a request may surface the browser UI); granted / denied = the user has decided.

Affordance

display reflects the current state; request offers to trigger the browser prompt (only meaningful from prompt); recover handles the denied reality — browsers block a programmatic re-prompt once denied, so recovery guides the user to browser/OS settings rather than firing the request again.

Feature

An open vocabulary keyed off the W3C PermissionName descriptors — extended as new powerful features ship, never enumerated closed.

Composition

Composed by Web Notifications (gating the “enable notifications” affordance) and by any camera/geolocation/clipboard consumer. The change event mirrors PermissionStatus.onchange.

name: System Notification Intent
summary: Protocol defining the OS-surface notification render — the platform Notifications API surface that renders OUTSIDE the page, survives the tab closing, and is gated by a notification permission. Sibling to the Feedback Intent (in-page transient toasts) and the Background Task Intent (in-session persistent surface), reusing the shared severity/title/body/action vocabulary. Explicitly NOT a surface dimension on Feedback — a distinct render target and lifecycle. Ratified in #009 Fork B; coordinated by the Web Notifications domain.

severity.description: Semantic weight of the notification — the SAME unified severity vocabulary shared with Feedback, Message, and Validation. Maps to the platform notification's icon/tone, never coined here.

interaction.description: Whether the notification carries action affordances. Borrows the Notifications API `actions` shape (action buttons that route through `notificationclick`).

persistence.description: How long the OS surface stays before auto-dismissing — the platform `requireInteraction` flag. `require-interaction` is the default when an action is present (parity with Feedback's `indefinite`-on-action accessibility rule).

description:
What This Is

The OS-surface render target: the browser/OS Notifications API surface that appears outside the page, survives the tab (or browser) closing, and is gated by an explicit notification permission. It is the render-out-of-page sibling of the two in-app notification surfaces.

Sibling surfaces — three distinct render targets, one vocabulary

  • Feedback — in-page, transient toasts that die with the page.
  • Background Task — in-session, a persistent surface that outlives a view but lives inside the app.
  • System Notification — out-of-page, the OS surface that survives the tab closing.

All three reuse the shared severity / title / body / action vocabulary, so a consumer can re-target the same message to a different surface without re-authoring it.

Not a Feedback dimension

This is deliberately not folded into Feedback as a surface: os value (#009 Fork B): the render target and the lifecycle differ fundamentally — an OS notification escapes the document, persists past tab close, and requires a permission grant Feedback never needs. A shared dimension would conflate two surfaces with incompatible lifecycles.

Permission gate

Showing an OS notification is gated by the Permission Intent with feature: notifications. This intent composes that state axis rather than re-implementing it — a denied permission routes to recovery, a prompt state offers the request affordance, and only granted permits a show.

Severity

Borrowed verbatim from the unified severity vocabulary (info / success / warning / error) shared across Feedback, Message, and Validation — mapped to the notification's icon and tone.

Interaction & Persistence

  • inert: a display-only notification; clicking focuses the originating page.
  • actionable: carries action buttons (the Notifications API actions array), routed through notificationclick.
  • auto-dismiss: the surface clears on the platform's own timeout.
  • require-interaction: stays until the user acts (the platform requireInteraction flag); the default when an action is present.

Composition (cross-reference, do not duplicate)

  • Permission: the gate — feature: notifications state, request, and recovery.
  • Feedback / Background Task: sibling surfaces sharing the message vocabulary; a single event may fan out to more than one surface.

Interface Protocol

type NotificationSeverity = 'info' | 'success' | 'warning' | 'error';
type NotificationInteraction = 'inert' | 'actionable';
type NotificationPersistence = 'auto-dismiss' | 'require-interaction';

interface SystemNotificationAction {
    label: string;            // shown as a notification action button
    intent: string;           // dispatched on notificationclick
}

interface SystemNotificationIntent {
    // Shared severity vocabulary → icon/tone
    severity: NotificationSeverity;
    title: string;
    body?: string;
    // Display-only vs. action buttons
    interaction: NotificationInteraction;
    // Maps to the platform requireInteraction flag
    persistence: NotificationPersistence;
    // Optional action affordances (require interaction: 'actionable')
    actions?: SystemNotificationAction[];
}

name: Notification Marker Intent
summary: Protocol defining the unread/attention MARKER family — the ambient count or dot shown on app chrome rather than in a message: the favicon badge, the document.title count prefix, and the OS app-icon badge (the Badging API `navigator.setAppBadge`). Models the count/clear lifecycle and per-surface graceful degradation (favicon + title everywhere; app-icon badge where supported). A marker, not a message — distinct from the Feedback / System Notification render surfaces. Spun out of #009; coordinated by the Web Notifications domain (#456) alongside system-notification.

surface.description: Which chrome surface carries the marker. An OPEN vocabulary — favicon and title work everywhere; the app-icon badge needs Badging API support and is the first to degrade. A consumer may light up several at once.

marker.description: What the marker conveys. `count` shows a number (capped, e.g. 9+); `flag` shows a presence dot with no number — the platform `setAppBadge()` called with no argument, and a dot overlay for favicon/title.

description:
What This Is

The marker family: an ambient unread/attention indicator painted on app chrome — not a message in a surface. Three render targets share one count/clear lifecycle:

  • favicon: a numbered/dotted overlay on the tab icon.
  • title: a count prefix on document.title (e.g. (3) Inbox).
  • app-badge: the OS app-icon badge via the Badging API (navigator.setAppBadge(n) / clearAppBadge()), shown on the installed PWA / dock / taskbar icon.

Marker, not message

Distinct from the Feedback and System Notification surfaces: those render a message; this only carries a count or a dot on existing chrome. A new unread item typically updates the marker and may fire a system notification — two intents composed, not merged.

Surface — graceful degradation

An open set, ordered by reach: favicon and title work in every browser (pure DOM); app-badge requires Badging API support (and usually an installed PWA), so it is the first to degrade. The intent's contract is that a consumer declares the count once and each available surface reflects it — an unsupported surface is silently skipped, never an error.

Marker

  • count: a number, capped for display (e.g. 9+); 0 clears the marker.
  • flag: a presence dot with no number — setAppBadge() with no argument, a dot overlay for favicon/title. For "something is unread" without a precise count.

Lifecycle

A single count/clear model drives every surface: set(count) paints all available surfaces; clear() (or set(0)) removes the marker everywhere. Idempotent — re-setting the same count is a no-op.

Composition (cross-reference, do not duplicate)

  • System Notification: the sibling out-of-page surface; a new item often updates this marker AND shows a notification.
  • Permission: favicon/title need none; the app-badge inherits the installed-PWA context but does not itself prompt.

Interface Protocol

type MarkerSurface = 'favicon' | 'title' | 'app-badge';
type MarkerKind = 'count' | 'flag';

interface NotificationMarkerIntent {
    // Which chrome surfaces to drive (open set; unsupported ones are skipped)
    surfaces: MarkerSurface[];
    // Count vs. presence dot
    marker: MarkerKind;
}

interface NotificationMarker {
    set(count: number): void;  // 0 clears; capped for display
    clear(): void;             // remove the marker on every surface
}

name: Web Identity Intent
summary: Thin, UX-only seam for credential mediation, a credential-request surface, and an auth-state signal (signed-in/out + identity descriptor). Composes the Loader intent (mediation in-flight) and the Feedback intent (auth-state change), anchored on the navigator.credentials.get mediation model and the typed credential union. No provider, ceremony, or protocol surface — those stay in the deferred webidentity project (#483).

mediation.description: How insistently the user is involved before a credential is returned. Mirrors the Credential Management API's mediation enum (navigator.credentials.get({ mediation })) one-to-one.

authState.description: The auth-state signal the seam emits. A declarative reflection of whether an identity is present — the consumer renders signed-in vs signed-out chrome from it, never from ad-hoc local state.

credential.description: The typed credential descriptor presented by the request surface. The UX-level projection of the native Credential union — which sign-in affordance to show — carrying no ceremony or protocol detail.

requestSurface.description: How the credential request is presented to the user. UX-only; the underlying get() call is identical across surfaces.

description:
What This Is

A thin, UX-only identity seam — three declarative affordances and nothing more:

  • Credential mediation: a declarative mediation hint that maps to navigator.credentials.get({ mediation }) — silent auto-resolves, conditional backs passkey autofill, required always prompts.
  • Credential-request surface: where the sign-in affordance is presented (inline / dialog / redirect) for a credential of a given type.
  • Auth-state signal: a reflected authState (signed-in/out + an identity descriptor) the consumer reads instead of inventing local session state.

Native anchor

Anchored on the platform Credential Management model: the mediation dimension is the native enum verbatim, and credential is the UX projection of the typed Credential union (PasswordCredential, FederatedCredential, PublicKeyCredential, plus the emerging identity descriptor). The intent deliberately stops at the request; it never models the provider, the ceremony, or any wire protocol.

Thin by design — what it is NOT

This is the UX-only seam graduated from #012's Fork 2-A. It carries no provider registry, no FedCM/WebAuthn ceremony surface, and no protocol — the full webidentity project + protocol stay deferred (#483). Consumers that need a ceremony compose the native API directly; this intent only standardizes the declarative request + state shape.

Composition (cross-reference, do not duplicate)

  • Loader: the pending auth-state and a non-silent mediation are an in-flight operation — drive the Loader intent for the spinner/skeleton, do not re-invent progress here.
  • Feedback: an authState transition (signed-in / signed-out) is announced through the Feedback intent, not a bespoke toast.

Near-term consumers

  • The live Guard presence-gate (#272) reads authState to gate a viewport.
  • The loan-app permission phase (#379) anchors its sign-in state to this shape instead of a local one.

Interface Protocol

type Mediation = 'silent' | 'optional' | 'conditional' | 'required';
type AuthState = 'unknown' | 'signed-out' | 'pending' | 'signed-in';
type CredentialType = 'password' | 'federated' | 'public-key' | 'identity';
type RequestSurface = 'inline' | 'dialog' | 'redirect';

interface IdentityDescriptor {
    // Opaque, UX-level identity label (name / handle / avatar) — never the raw credential
    label: string;
    avatarUrl?: string;
}

interface WebIdentityIntent {
    // Declarative mediation hint -> navigator.credentials.get({ mediation })
    mediation: Mediation;
    // Which credential affordance the request surface presents
    credential: CredentialType;
    // How the request is surfaced
    requestSurface: RequestSurface;
}

interface WebIdentitySignal {
    // Reflected auth-state the consumer renders from
    readonly state: AuthState;
    // Present only when state === 'signed-in'
    readonly identity?: IdentityDescriptor;
}

name: Expressive Symbol Intent
summary: A content/meaningful glyph (emoji or custom-image sticker) distinct from the UI-affordance icon — owns its accessible-name dimension, carries the Unicode skin-tone/presentation dimensions, and a default-less render-strategy platform config. Substrate for the picker + reaction consumers (#370).

mode.description: Symbol source — a Unicode emoji glyph or a custom-image sticker (label required).

accessibleName.description: Whether the symbol is part of the message (role=img + accessible name) or purely decorative (opt-in suppression). Default meaningful.

skinTone.description: Unicode emoji modifier (Fitzpatrick) for an emoji-modifier-base glyph — emoji mode only.

presentation.description: Variation selector: text (VS15 U+FE0E, monochrome) vs emoji (VS16 U+FE0F, color) — emoji mode only.

renderStrategy.description: Platform-config paint strategy (default-less, inherited from the platform flavor): native emoji font vs a vendored image set.

description:
What This Is

A content/meaningful glyph — an emoji or sticker that is part of the message, as distinct from the Icon intent's UI-affordance glyph. Sibling of Icon, graduated from decision #370 Fork 2. The substrate the general picker surface and the reaction block both compose.

Modes

  • emoji: a Unicode emoji glyph. Carries the skin-tone and presentation Unicode dimensions; an accessible name can fall back to the CLDR short name.
  • sticker: a custom image (project-supplied asset). A label is required — there is no CLDR fallback for a non-Unicode glyph; an unlabeled meaningful sticker is invalid.

Accessible name (owned dimension)

This intent owns its accessible-name concern rather than borrowing it: a meaningful symbol renders as role="img" with an accessible name (emoji → CLDR short-name fallback; sticker → the required explicit label), while a decorative symbol is the opt-in case (aria-hidden / empty name). Default is meaningful — the most descriptive value; suppression is the author's deliberate opt-in.

Unicode dimensions (emoji mode)

  • skinTone: the Unicode emoji modifier (Fitzpatrick EMOJI MODIFIER FITZPATRICK TYPE 1-2 … 6) applied to an emoji-modifier-base glyph.
  • presentation: the variation selector — text (VS15, U+FE0E) or emoji (VS16, U+FE0F) — selecting monochrome text vs full-color emoji rendering.

Render strategy (platform config — default-less)

How a symbol is painted is a platform-configuration dimension, not a per-use prop: native-font (the OS/browser emoji font) or image-set (a vendored emoji-image set — e.g. Twemoji/Noto — identified by a vendor). Per #370 Web Everything mandates no default: renderStrategy is inherited from the active platform flavor and extended by the project config, never hard-coded by the intent.

Interface Protocol

type SymbolMode = 'emoji' | 'sticker';type AccessibleName = 'meaningful' | 'decorative';type SkinTone = 'default' | 'light' | 'medium-light' | 'medium' | 'medium-dark' | 'dark';type Presentation = 'text' | 'emoji';type RenderStrategy = 'native-font' | 'image-set';interface ExpressiveSymbolIntent {    // Symbol source: a Unicode emoji or a custom-image sticker    mode: SymbolMode;    // The glyph identifier (emoji code point sequence) or the sticker asset id    symbol: string;    // Required when mode === 'sticker'; the CLDR short name backs the emoji case    label?: string;    // Meaningful (role=img + name) vs decorative (aria-hidden). Default 'meaningful'    accessibleName?: AccessibleName;    // Unicode dimensions — emoji mode only    skinTone?: SkinTone;    presentation?: Presentation;}// Platform config (default-less, inherited from the platform flavor) — NOT a per-use propinterface ExpressiveSymbolRenderConfig {    renderStrategy: RenderStrategy;    // Required when renderStrategy === 'image-set' (e.g. 'twemoji', 'noto')    vendor?: string;}

name: Reaction Intent
summary: The reaction interaction (toggle + aggregate + roster) — the consumer end of the expressive-symbol seam. Adopts Atlassian ReactionSummary as the data-contract baseline (count · users[] · reacted · optimisticallyUpdated); not a protocol; multi-user sync is a composed webrealtime concern, never baked in (#370 Fork 5).

reactionSet.description: Which symbols are offerable — the convergent sentiment default, a project named-set, or the full all-emojis picker (default-less core; sentiment flavor default).

roster.description: How the who-reacted list surfaces — just the aggregate count, or a keyboard-reachable, display-limited roster of names.

optimistic.description: Whether your own toggle updates immediately before the round-trip confirms, or waits for confirmation.

description:
What This Is

The reaction interaction — affix a symbol to content, toggle your own, aggregate a count, and show a who-reacted roster. The consumer end of the provider→consumer seam (decision #370 Fork 5): it composes the expressive-symbol glyph, it never re-renders it.

The contract — ReactionSummary (baseline)

Adopts Atlassian's ReactionSummary as the contract baseline: count (the aggregate) · users[] (the who-reacted roster, with a display limit) · reacted (toggle-your-own) · optimisticallyUpdated. Not a protocol — a data contract with no swappable-vendor/engine-interop story (minimize lock-in). Multi-user sync is composed, never baked in: "others see your reaction" is a separate real-time concern driven through live-region-status + a webrealtime protocol.

Dimensions

  • reactionSet: which symbols are offerable — sentiment (the convergent default set), a project named-set, or all-emojis (the full-picker escape). Default-less core; sentiment is the flavor default (Fork 6).
  • roster: how the who-reacted list surfaces — count-only (just the aggregate) or names (a keyboard-reachable roster, display-limited).
  • optimistic: whether your own toggle updates immediately (on) before the round-trip confirms, or waits (off).

Accessibility (load-bearing)

Each reaction is an aria-pressed toggle with a composed accessible name — emoji name + count + your-reacted-state ("thumbs up, 5 reactions, you reacted, activate to remove"), not a bare glyph. Announce your local toggle only via live-region-status — never every remote tick (which would be a screen-reader flood). The roster is keyboard-reachable.

vs. the status badge

A reaction count chip resembles the surface-tokened badge of #354, but means "N people reacted," not "this state" — they compose, they do not merge.

name: Inline Trigger Intent
summary: The declarative dimensions of the inline trigger-character picker (#591): an open trigger-set registry plus boundary, query, and insertion-mode axes, all most-permissive by default. Pairs with the inline-trigger block (the runnable detection engine). UX-only — names what the author configures, not how detection is implemented.

triggerSet.description: Which trigger characters open the picker — an open config registry the project extends (@/:/#// are conventions, never a fixed enum); each maps to a results provider

boundaryPolicy.description: What anchors a valid trigger; most-permissive default is any-prefix anywhere, the restriction is the author's opt-in (startOfLine / allowedPrefixes)

queryPolicy.description: How the query slice after the trigger is bounded; default is unbounded, the author opts into limits (minQueryLength / maxLength / allowSpaces)

insertionMode.description: Which reference insertion adapter commits the resolved selection {key, query, range, item} — detection-only, so the standard owns neither commit (Fork 2-A)

description:
What This Is

The declarative dimensions of the inline trigger-character picker ratified in #591 — the surface that watches an editable region, detects a boundary-anchored trigger char (@/:/#//), slices the query, anchors a popover to the caret, and routes results through a listbox. Pairs with the Inline Trigger block (the runnable detection engine). UX-only: it names what an author configures, never how detection is implemented.

Dimensions

  • triggerSet: which trigger chars are live — an open config registry the project extends (@ people, : emoji, # reference, / command are conventions, never a hardcoded enum), each bound to a results provider.
  • boundaryPolicy: what anchors a valid trigger — any-prefix (the most-permissive default), start-of-line, or allowed-prefixes-only. The restriction is the author's opt-in.
  • queryPolicy: how the query slice is bounded — unbounded by default; min-length / max-length / no-spaces are opt-in limits.
  • insertionMode: which reference adapter commits the resolved selection — text (default; replaces the query range with a string) or token (composes the rich-text standard #590). Detection-only (Fork 2-A): the standard emits {key, query, range, item} and owns neither commit.

Forced invariants (not dimensions)

Virtual focus — DOM focus stays on the editable region while the listbox is navigated via aria-activedescendant (WAI-ARIA APG editable combobox); a roving tabindex would steal focus from the text and is broken. IME-composition suspension — detection pauses while a CJK IME is composing. Not a protocol — a deterministic transform with no swappable-vendor interop story.

Composition

Caret anchoring composes the anchor intent over a virtual caret element; results compose the picker surface / autocomplete; match counts announce through live-region-status. The native-first baseline degrades to a plain textbox with no inline suggestions.

name: Hover Intent
summary: A standalone trigger-transition concern (ratified #609 Fork 2A): open/close a revealed surface on hover with INTENT — tolerate diagonal trigger→panel travel and don't open on an incidental pass-through. UX-only and orthogonal to the anchor intent's placement/dismissal; composed by reveal-nav, menu submenus, and hovercards. The technical mechanic (delay vs CSS safe-area vs JS pointer polygon) is NOT here — it's a Technical Configurator card.

openIntent.description: How deliberate a hover must be to open — deliberate ignores an incidental pass-through (the sensible default); eager opens the moment the pointer enters

travelForgiveness.description: Whether the pointer may cut diagonally from the trigger across to the panel without closing it — forgiving tolerates the diagonal corridor (default); strict closes on any pointer-leave

touch.description: The touch fallback, since hover has no touch equivalent — tap-toggle (first tap opens, the most-permissive default) or none (hover-only, pointer devices)

description:
What This Is

A small, standalone trigger-transition concern ratified in #609 (Fork 2A): opening and closing a revealed surface on hover with intent — tolerate diagonal travel from the trigger to the panel, and don't open on an incidental pass-through. It is orthogonal to the anchor intent (placement + dismissal) — a reveal composes both: anchor positions the panel, hover-intent decides when the hover counts. Composed by reveal-nav, menu submenus, and hovercards.

Dimensions (UX-only)

  • openIntent: deliberate (ignore an incidental pass-through — the sensible default) vs eager (open on enter).
  • travelForgiveness: forgiving (tolerate the diagonal trigger→panel corridor — default) vs strict (close on pointer-leave).
  • touch: tap-toggle (the most-permissive touch fallback — first tap opens) vs none.

UX-only → the mechanic is a Configurator card

The how — delay-only timers vs a CSS safe-area bridge vs a JS pointer-direction safe-triangle polygon — is a technical strategy, not part of this intent. It is authored as a Technical Configurator domain (open-delay / close-delay / safe-area strategy / touch) in plateau-app, per intent-UX-only→configurator. Grounded in the reveal-nav design sweep (/research/design-sweep-navigation-menus/).

name: Flow Progress Intent
summary: Canonical, accessible presentation of where a user is in a multi-step workflow — the current position, each step's status, and what remains before completion — so a wizard, a single-page progressive form, a conversational flow, and a board all read consistently. The UX member of the Web Workflows protocol: it reads the same workflow engine and renders the position + per-step status, while the orchestration (transitions, guards, parallel, completion) stays on the protocol. Reuses navigation's structure:linear / guard / history rather than reinventing them, and adopts the native aria-current="step" token. Avoid the name 'stepper' — it collides with the numeric NumberField.

register.description: The visual arrangement of the flow — all coherent end-states (support-all); the most-permissive default is the richest, dedicated-surface wizard

stepStatus.description: How each step's state is surfaced (the cleanest surveyed enum; `disabled` and `optional` are orthogonal modifiers, not members)

movement.description: Which traversal the UX affords — reuses navigation's guard axis; back/undo is allowed by default (most-permissive), forward-only is the author's opt-in

description:
What Flow Progress Is

The canonical, accessible presentation of where a user is in a workflow — the checkout that is on pay after collect-address, the onboarding on step 3 of 5, the application part-way through a multi-stage review. It is the UX member of the Web Workflows protocol: it reads the same workflow engine and renders the current position plus each step's status, while the orchestration (transitions, guards, parallel, completion) lives on the protocol.

Reuse, don't reinvent

This intent does not re-create linear navigation. It reuses the navigation intent's structure:linear, guard, and history dimensions, and adopts the native aria-current="step" token for the position indicator. Per-step status uses the cleanest surveyed enum (wait / process / finish / error), with disabled and optional as orthogonal modifiers.

Dimensions (UX-only)

  • register: wizard (dedicated-surface, the most-permissive default) / single-page (progressive disclosure) / conversational / board — all coherent end-states, a configurable dimension, never a fork.
  • stepStatus: wait / process / finish / error.
  • movement: back-allowed (default) / free / forward-only — the back/undo affordance, reusing navigation's guard axis.

UX-only → orchestration is the Workflow protocol

The machinery — the transition graph, guards, parallel fork/join, the runnable engine — is not part of this intent (intents carry no impl refs). It is the Workflow protocol, resolved by a swappable CustomWorkflowEngine. Ratified in #634; grounded in the workflow meta-intent survey (/research/workflow-meta-intent/).

name: Text-Formatting Controls
summary: The formatting-controls axis (ratified #590 Fork 5): which format operations a surface exposes, where the controls sit, and how they overflow. UX-only and editor-independent — the same axis governs a comment box, a rich-text surface, or a markdown field — so it earns its own home apart from the rich-text surface intent. Composes droplist (grouped op menus), popover (floating bubble), and button (single ops). The technical mechanic (execCommand vs a command registry vs a custom serializer) is NOT here — it is a Technical Configurator concern.

exposure.description: Which formatting operations are surfaced. minimal = inline emphasis + link; standard = + lists, headings, blockquote (the recommended default); full = + block insertion, tables, media; custom = an author-declared op set.

placement.description: Where the controls live relative to the surface. toolbar = a persistent bar (the discoverable default); floating = a selection bubble anchored to the current range; inline = in-content affordances (slash menu / markdown shortcuts); contextual = a right-click / long-press menu.

overflow.description: What happens when the controls exceed the available width. overflow-menu = collapse extras behind a "more" affordance (the most-permissive default — nothing is lost); scroll = a horizontally scrollable strip; wrap = flow onto a second row.

description:
What This Is

The formatting-controls axis ratified in #590 (Fork 5): which format operations a surface exposes, where the controls sit, and how they overflow. It is UX-only and editor-independent — the same axis governs a plain comment box, a rich-text surface (see rich-text), or a markdown field — which is why it earns its own home rather than being baked into the editing surface.

Dimensions (UX-only)

  • exposure: minimal (inline emphasis + link) → standard (recommended default — + lists, headings, blockquote) → full (+ block insertion, tables, media) → custom (author-declared op set).
  • placement: toolbar (persistent bar — the discoverable default) vs floating (selection bubble) vs inline (slash menu / markdown shortcuts) vs contextual (right-click / long-press).
  • overflow: overflow-menu (collapse behind a “more” affordance — the most-permissive default, nothing is lost) vs scroll (horizontal strip) vs wrap (second row).

Composition

Controls compose existing intents: droplist-style grouped op menus, anchored floating bubbles, and single-op buttons. A control reflects the current selection state (active / inactive / disabled) and applies an operation to the active range.

UX-only → the mechanic is a Configurator card

The how a format operation is realized — legacy execCommand vs a declarative command registry vs a custom serializer round-trip — is a technical strategy, not part of this intent. It is authored as a Technical Configurator domain, per intent-UX-only→configurator.

name: Rich-Text Surface
summary: The editable rich-text surface UX (ratified #590, webediting #618): an editable / read-only surface that hosts flowed, formatted content. Negotiates the editing-surface capabilities (contenteditable floor + editcontext upgrade, #590 Fork 3), sanitizes incoming content to its allowed schema (Sanitizer API), and decorates ranges without mutating the model (Highlight API). The controls that drive it are a separate axis — see text-formatting. The engine that backs it is a Technical Configurator / Editor Engine protocol concern, not part of this UX intent.

mode.description: Whether the surface accepts input. editable = the surface is editable (the default); read-only = the same content model is rendered without an editing affordance (a published view of authored content).

flow.description: The content shape the surface permits. multiline = block-flowed content with paragraphs and block elements (the most-permissive default); singleline = a constrained single line of inline-formatted text (no block breaks) — e.g. a styled title field.

paste.description: How externally-pasted content is admitted, borrowing Sanitizer API vocabulary. sanitized = clean incoming HTML to the surface’s allowed schema (the safe default); plain-text = strip all formatting to text; raw = admit as-is (author-trusted source only).

description:
What This Is

The editable rich-text surface UX ratified in #590 for the webediting project: a surface that hosts flowed, formatted content and can be editable or read-only. The controls that drive formatting are a separate axis — see text-formatting — because a formatting toolbar recurs without an editor.

Dimensions (UX-only)

  • mode: editable (default) vs read-only (the same model rendered without an editing affordance).
  • flow: multiline (block-flowed paragraphs — the most-permissive default) vs singleline (one constrained line, inline formatting only).
  • paste: sanitized (clean to the allowed schema via the Sanitizer API — the safe default) vs plain-text (strip to text) vs raw.

Capabilities It Negotiates

The surface requiresCapabilities the four editing-surface capabilities (#628): a contenteditable floor, an EditContext upgrade (#590 Fork 3), the Sanitizer API for paste, and the Highlight API to decorate ranges (spellcheck / collaboration cursors) without mutating the model.

UX-only → the engine is a protocol

The engine that maintains the content model, applies operations, and serializes — is the Editor Engine protocol / a Technical Configurator concern, not part of this UX intent.
